title: Livestock insurance for smallholders
subtitle: What to cover, what policies typically exclude, and whether it's worth it.
excerpt: Livestock insurance covers sudden death, theft, and straying, and some policies extend to vet fees. Whether it pays off depends on the value of your animals, your proximity to other livestock holdings, and how much vet-fee exposure you can absorb without a policy. This is a framework for working through those questions.
sourceNotes: NFU Mutual smallholder insurance guidance, nfumutual.co.uk. British Livestock Insurance Group market overview. Association of British Insurers (ABI) farm insurance guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: Insurance that pays the market value of an animal at the time of loss, rather than a fixed sum agreed at the policy start. Relevant for animals whose value fluctuates (breeding stock, competition animals).

content.text: Livestock insurance is a simple product once you know what it covers and what it doesn't. The decision for a small holding comes down to three questions. What is the replacement value of the animals? What vet fees could you face if a high-value animal falls ill? How likely are the insured events in your situation?
Theft of livestock from the holding.
Straying, the cost of rounding up animals that escape onto a road or neighbouring property.
Death from disease, depending on the policy tier.
Pre-existing conditions, any disease the animal had before the policy start.
Negligence: deaths caused by poor fencing, water failure, or poisonous plants left in the field.
APHA compulsory slaughter: this is covered by state compensation, not your policy.
Normal losses: deaths from difficult births or standard illness are usually excluded.
Policy value is set either at a fixed sum or at

at the time of claim. For commercial breeding stock or pedigree animals, market value cover is usually more appropriate. For commercial slaughter pigs or finishing lambs, a fixed sum per head often aligns better with realistic replacement cost.

For most small holdings with fewer than twenty livestock animals, a basic policy may not be cost-effective when weighed against the premium. The calculation changes if you keep high-value breeding stock (a registered dairy goat, a pedigree boar), or if your fencing is close to a main road. The straying liability clause often has more practical value than the death cover on a small mixed holding.

Most farm policies include public liability cover for escaped livestock on a road. For most small holdings, this matters more than the mortality cover. A single road accident caused by a strayed animal can cost far more than replacing the animal.
